1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	
4	Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $k$ in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus $g = g(C)$.
5	
6	Because the Picard-scheme machinery (FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$ and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites) is blocked at the level of Mathlib infrastructure (see Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A below for the detailed gap analysis), this chapter builds the Jacobian by an alternative ad-hoc route: the Albanese functor on smooth proper curves.
7	
8	\section{The Albanese construction}
9	
10	For a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$, the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from $C$. Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
11	
12	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
13	
14	Let $C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)$ be the $g$\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of $C$ (the quotient of $C^g$ by the action of the symmetric group $S_g$). It is a smooth proper scheme over $k$ of dimension $g$. Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
15	\[
16	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
17	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
18	\]
19	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing $P_0$ gives a translation isomorphism $\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}$, and the Stein factorisation of $\sigma$ yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
20	
21	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of $C$ inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
22	
23	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
24	
25	\begin{definition}\leanok
26	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
27	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
28	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
29	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
30	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
31	  % such $b$ arises from a unique map $a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}$ such that
32	  % $a(0)=0$.  Since $B^*$ is integral, $a$ factors through $P$.  Thus the
33	  % maps $a\:B^*\to P$ and $b\:X\to B$ are in bijective correspondence.
34	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps $b'\:B\to B'$ such
35	  % that $b'(0)=0$.  In particular, $1_P$ corresponds to a natural map
36	  % $u\:X\to A$ such that $u(x)=0$, and every map $b\:X\to B$ factors
37	  % uniquely through $u$."
38	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
39	  Let $(C, P)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with a marked $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$. A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ is an \emph{Albanese object} for $(C, P)$ if there exists a morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ sending $P$ to the identity element $\eta \in J$, and such that every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ factors uniquely as $f = \iota \circ g$ for some morphism of group schemes $g \colon J \to A$.
40	\end{definition}
41	
42	\begin{remark}
43	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
44	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
45	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on $J$ --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on $\iota$ and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on $J$ sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on $\Jac(C)$ before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety $A$ in the universal-property clause.
46	\end{remark}
47	
48	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
49	
50	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term $h$ packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition $P \circ \iota = \eta_J$ and the universal-factorisation property of $\iota$. The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
51	
52	\begin{definition}
53	
54	\leanok
55	[The Albanese universal morphism]
56	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
58	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
59	  Given a pointed curve $(C, P)$ and an Albanese term $h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$, the \emph{universal morphism} of $h$ is the morphism
60	  \[
61	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
62	  \]
63	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of $h$.
64	\end{definition}
65

113	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
114	
115	\begin{definition}\leanok
116	[Jacobian]
117	  \label{def:Jacobian}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
119	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
120	  The \emph{Jacobian} of $C$ is the abelian variety $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ obtained as the Albanese object of $C$: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ of dimension $g(C)$ equipped with a universal morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$. Formally, $\Jac(C)$ is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-$P$) Albanese witness for $C$ provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
121	
122	  When $g(C) = 0$ the Jacobian collapses to $\Spec k$ itself, which is the terminal object in the category of $k$-schemes. When $g(C) > 0$ the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers $C$ itself (after choosing a $k$-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: $\Jac(C)$ is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-$0$ specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over $k$ of relative dimension $0$ is isomorphic to $\Spec k$.
123	\end{definition}

125	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
126	
127	The four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.

129	\begin{theorem}\leanok
130	[Group object structure]
131	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
133	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
134	  $\Jac(C)$ carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	\begin{proof}
138	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
139	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, $\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J$ as a $k$-scheme, where $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$ is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field $\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J$, so $\Jac(C)$ inherits it by projection.
140	
141	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if $\iota \colon C \to J$ is the universal morphism sending the marked point $P$ to the identity, then the diagonal $C \times C \to J \times J$, post-composed with the universal morphism out of $C \times C$ (factored through $J$ by Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
142	\end{proof}

231	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
232	
233	\begin{itemize}
234	  \item $g = 0$: Then $\Jac(C) = \Spec k$, the trivial group scheme; $\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $0 = g$.
235	  \item $g = 1$: Then $\Jac(C) \cong C$ once a $k$-rational point on $C$ is fixed; $C$ is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension $1$).
236	  \item $g \ge 2$: $\Jac(C)$ is a non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to $C$ as a scheme.
237	\end{itemize}

239	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
240	
241	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: $\Jac(C)$ is the connected component of the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. An equivalent route, available whenever a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ and translate by $-gP$.
242	
243	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
244	
245	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of $C$. This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
246	
247	\begin{definition}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[Albanese witness for a curve]
251	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
253	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
254	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ is a bundle of the following seven fields:
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item $J$: an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$ --- the candidate Albanese object.
257	    \item $\mathtt{grpObj}$: a group-object structure on $J$ in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
258	    \item $\mathtt{proper}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is proper.
259	    \item $\mathtt{smooth}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth.
260	    \item $\mathtt{geomIrred}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is geometrically irreducible.
261	    \item $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$. This refines $\mathtt{smooth}$ at the specific relative dimension $\genus\, C$ (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
262	    \item $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$: a proof that, for every $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
263	  \end{itemize}
264	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
265	\end{definition}
266	
267	\begin{remark}
268	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
269	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
270	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
271	  \[
272	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
273	  \]
274	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single $J$ is chosen once (intrinsic to $C$), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
275	  \[
276	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
277	  \]
278	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to $C$: a different choice of $P \in C(k)$ alters the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism of $J$, and so leaves $J$ unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-$P$ projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, and $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ each take a marked point $P$ as input and project the corresponding component of $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P$ via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).

Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme $J$; only the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ varies with the marked point $P$. We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
312	
313	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a $P$-independent witness.}
314	  The witness $J$ depends only on $C$, not on the marked point $P$. Indeed, both classical constructions below produce $J$ as an intrinsic object of $C$ (the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$, or the Stein-factorisation target of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ translated by a fixed $P_0$); a different choice of $P$ in $C(k)$ alters $\iota_P$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism, and so does not change $J$ as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling $J$ as a single field and the universal-property data as $\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J$. We now turn to producing $J$.

316	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
317	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
318	
319	  \begin{itemize}
320	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a $k$-scheme $T$, write $C_T := C \times_k T$ and let $\pi \colon C_T \to T$ be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
321	    \[
322	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
323	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
324	    \]
325	    A $T$-point is a line bundle on $C_T$ modulo a line bundle pulled back from $T$. The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on $\Pic(C_T)$ descends to the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ and gives $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ a natural group-functor refinement.
326	
327	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For $C$ smooth proper geometrically connected over $k$ with a $k$-rational point, $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ (after \'etale sheafification, if $k$ is not algebraically closed) is representable by a $k$-group scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k$, which is needed for the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ to be \'etale-locally injective.
328	
329	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ be the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$. As the identity component of a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is geometrically irreducible; for $C$ smooth proper of genus $g$, it is moreover a smooth proper $k$-group scheme of dimension $g$. Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$ is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the kernel of the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$, which is locally constant). Take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
330	
331	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each $P \in C(k)$, the Abel--Jacobi morphism
332	    \[
333	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
334	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
335	    \]
336	    sends $P$ to the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. It is universal among morphisms $f \colon C \to A$ to smooth proper geometrically irreducible $k$-group schemes $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$: this is the classical Albanese property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, proved by reducing to the case $k = \overline k$ (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on $C \times A$ trivialised along $\{P\} \times A$ correspond bijectively to morphisms $C \to A^{\vee} = A$ sending $P$ to $\eta_A$.

350	  This route follows Milne's \emph{Abelian Varieties} (Chapter~III) and avoids FGA representability at the cost of requiring quotients of schemes by finite group actions.

352	  \begin{itemize}
353	    \item[\textbf{(B.1)}] \emph{The $g$-fold symmetric power.} Form the product $C^g$ and let $S_g$ act by permutation of the factors. The categorical quotient $C^{(g)} := C^g / S_g$ exists as a $k$-scheme, smooth and proper over $k$ of dimension $g$. Smoothness follows because the action of $S_g$ on the smooth $k$-scheme $C^g$ is free outside the diagonal locus and the local-ring computation at the diagonal points produces a smooth ring (this is the symmetric-product computation of Fogarty / Mumford). Properness descends from properness of $C^g$ via the geometric-quotient property.
354	
355	    \item[\textbf{(B.2)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi morphism on symmetric powers.} Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
356	    \[
357	      \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
358	      \sum_{i=1}^g [Q_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g Q_i\bigr)

359	    \]
360	    is well-defined (the right-hand side is symmetric in the $Q_i$ and depends only on the unordered tuple) and is surjective. By Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, $\sigma$ is birational onto its image: a generic divisor of degree $g$ on $C$ has $h^0(\mathcal O_C(D)) = 1$, so the fibre $\sigma^{-1}([\mathcal O_C(D)])$ is a point at generic divisors; the special fibres are projective spaces $\mathbb P(h^0 - 1)$ over the locus where $h^0 > 1$.
361	
362	    \item[\textbf{(B.3)}] \emph{The Stein factorisation.} Because $\sigma$ is a proper surjection with geometrically connected fibres (each fibre $\mathbb P^{h^0-1}_k$ is geometrically irreducible), its Stein factorisation
363	    \[
364	      C^{(g)} \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_*\;} \Spec_{\Pic^g_{C/k}}\bigl(\sigma_* \mathcal O_{C^{(g)}}\bigr) \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}
365	    \]
366	    presents the second arrow as a finite morphism whose source is the intermediate scheme. Since the fibres of $\sigma$ are connected, this intermediate scheme equals $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ scheme-theoretically (the Stein factorisation is trivial on the second arrow when fibres are connected, by Stein's classical theorem). The middle scheme is therefore $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ itself, which is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of dimension $g$. Translating by $-g P_0$ yields $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, and we take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as in Route A. The universal property is then established as in Sub-step A.4.
367	  \end{itemize}

376	  \paragraph{Genus-$0$ sub-case (absorbed into the same witness).}
377	  When $g(C) = 0$ the witness's underlying scheme is $J = \Spec k$, and the universal property reduces to a rigidity statement.

379	  \begin{itemize}
380	    \item[\textbf{(C.1)}] \emph{Brauer--Severi identification.} A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ with $g(C) = 0$ and a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_k$. This is the Brauer--Severi triviality criterion: a Brauer--Severi variety of dimension $1$ is $\mathbb P^1$ over its base if and only if it has a rational point. The genus-$0$ identification on the curve side uses $h^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ (Riemann--Roch) to produce a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_k$, which is then an isomorphism by degree-and-dimension.
381	
382	    \item[\textbf{(C.2)}] \emph{Rigidity for morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety.} We state the rigidity result over the algebraic closure $\bar k$, since the protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does not assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ and the genus-$0$ identification $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ is only available after passing to $\bar k$ (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexamples to a $k$-side identification without a rational point). The conclusion over $k$ --- equality of the given morphism with the constant morphism as $k$-schemes --- then follows by \emph{descent}: a morphism equality that holds after base change to $\bar k$ descends to $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (Sub-step~C.2.f).
383	
384	    \begin{itemize}
385	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.a)}] \emph{Statement (over $\bar k$).} Let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$, with identity element $\eta_A \in A(\bar k)$. Let
386	      \[
387	        f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \longrightarrow A
388	      \]
389	      be a morphism of $\bar k$-schemes such that $f(p) = \eta_A$ for some closed point $p \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}(\bar k)$. Then $f$ factors as the composite $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A$; equivalently, $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.
390	
391	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let $c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ denote the constant morphism at $\eta_A$ --- the unique $\bar k$-morphism factoring through the closed-point section $\eta_A \colon \Spec \bar k \hookrightarrow A$. The conclusion $f = c$ follows from Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} applied to $g_1 := f$, $g_2 := c$, $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, $Y := A$, once we produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $U.\iota \circ f = U.\iota \circ c$ as scheme morphisms. (After the iter-125 refactor, the cited theorem is stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated; the source-side group-object decoration that the earlier formulation carried has been dropped. $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ satisfies the refactored hypotheses trivially --- it is smooth, hence reduced; it is geometrically irreducible over $\bar k$; and $A$ is proper hence separated over $\bar k$.) The reduction therefore amounts to: produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $f$ and $c$ agree as scheme morphisms; we will in fact take $U = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
392	
393	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism $f$ goes from a proper geometrically integral $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $1$ to a smooth proper $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $g := \dim_{\bar k} A$. The set-theoretic image $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A$ is an irreducible closed subset of $A$ (the continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map is irreducible and closed) of dimension at most $1$. If $g = 0$ then $A = \Spec \bar k$ and the conclusion is vacuous: the unique $\bar k$-morphism to $\Spec \bar k$ is the constant morphism. Henceforth assume $g \geq 1$, in which case the image is either a single point (the desired conclusion) or a one-dimensional irreducible proper closed subscheme of $A$ --- an irreducible rational curve in $A$.
394	
395	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Key classical input: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- image of dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by the following classical fact (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4, Proposition):
396	
397	      \begin{quote}
398	        \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}
399	      \end{quote}
400	
401	      Two standard proofs are available; both unwind a few layers of upstream Mathlib infrastructure but are routine once the input lemmas are present.
402	      \begin{itemize}
403	        \item \emph{Via the dual abelian variety.} Pullback of line bundles along $f$ induces a homomorphism of group schemes $f^{\vee} \colon A^{\vee} \to \Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$, where $A^{\vee}$ is the dual abelian variety (the moduli space of degree-zero line bundles on $A$). The Picard group of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ has trivial degree-zero component, $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, because every line bundle on $\mathbb P^1$ of degree $0$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}$ (Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6 and II.7). Hence $f^{\vee} = 0$. By autoduality of abelian varieties ($A^{\vee\vee} = A$ canonically, double duality of the Poincar\'e line bundle), the zero of $f^{\vee}$ implies that $f$ itself is constant.
404	
405	        \item \emph{Via the cotangent bundle.} The cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ of an abelian variety is a trivial $\mathcal O_A$-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along $f$ yields a morphism of locally free sheaves $f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}$, whose global sections form a $\bar k$-linear map
406	        \[
407	          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
408	        \]
409	        Triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ means the left-hand side is a non-zero $\bar k$-vector space (of dimension $g$); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential $df$ to vanish identically on $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. Smoothness of $A$ over $\bar k$ together with the vanishing of $df$ forces $f$ to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism.
410	      \end{itemize}
411	      Either proof yields: $f$ is constant. The pointed condition $f(p) = \eta_A$ then identifies the constant value, so $f = c$ on underlying topological points.
412	
413	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promoting set-level equality to scheme equality.} The set-level equality $f = c$ on underlying topological points (established in C.2.d) promotes to scheme-morphism equality on any non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via the standard reduced-source / separated-target argument: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is reduced (smooth implies regular, regular implies reduced) and $A$ is separated over $\bar k$ (proper implies separated), so a $\bar k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is determined by its values on the underlying topological space. In particular, $f|_U = c|_U$ as scheme morphisms for the open $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ itself, and the reduction of C.2.b gives $f = c$ as scheme morphisms.
414

417	      Concretely, let $f, c \colon C \to A$ be two $k$-morphisms (in the application $f$ is the given map and $c$ the constant map at $\eta_A$). Base-change along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ produces $f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k} \colon C_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ together with the projections $p \colon C_{\bar k} = C \times_k \bar k \to C$ and $q \colon A_{\bar k} \to A$, fitting the base-change squares $q \circ f_{\bar k} = f \circ p$ and $q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$. Suppose $f_{\bar k} = c_{\bar k}$ (this is what \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} delivers over $\bar k$). Then $f \circ p = q \circ f_{\bar k} = q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$, so $f$ and $c$ agree after pre-composition with $p$.

419	      It remains to right-cancel $p$. The morphism $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ is faithfully flat (a field extension is a faithfully flat ring map) and surjective; both properties are stable under base change, so the projection $p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C$ is faithfully flat and surjective. By \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}, $p$ is therefore an epimorphism of schemes, and right-cancelling it in $f \circ p = c \circ p$ gives $f = c$ over $k$. This is the entire descent: a faithfully-flat surjective cover induces an epimorphism, hence the base-change map is injective on the $\mathrm{Hom}$-set into $A$. The step is a $\sim 2$-line consequence of the named Mathlib lemma plus base-change-square commutativity, and is cheaper than the proper-cohomology flat-base-change machinery a cohomological descent would have required.

448	\label{sec:genusZeroWitness}
449	
450	The genus-$0$ sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} admits an explicit witness construction at the Lean level, packaged separately from the general existence statement so that the genus-$0$ branch can be discharged independently once the shared cotangent-vanishing pile lands (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii)).

452	\begin{theorem}\leanok
453	[Genus-$0$ Albanese witness]
454	  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
456	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar, def:genus}
457	  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is $\Spec k$ (the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $0$, properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field encodes the Albanese universal property for every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$.
458	\end{theorem}
459	
460	\begin{proof}
461	  \uses{thm:rigidity_over_kbar, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, def:genus}
462	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
463	
464	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take $J := \mathbf{1}$, the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, which on underlying schemes is $\Spec k$ viewed as a $k$-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
465	
466	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to $\mathbf{1}$, and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism $\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}$ is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:120}--\texttt{126}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on $\Spec k$ from the fact that, on the geometric fibre $\Spec K$ over any field extension $k \to K$, the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and $\Spec K$ is irreducible.
467	
468	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension $\genus\,C$.} The smoothness witness $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ is required at relative dimension $\genus\,C$. The hypothesis $\genus\,C = 0$ rewrites the target relative dimension to $0$, after which the goal reduces to $\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})$. This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension $0$.
469	
470	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$, we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple $(C, P, J)$.
471	
472	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map $\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}$ (the structure morphism of $C$ as a $k$-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition $P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}$ holds automatically: both sides are morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
473	
474	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with identity element $\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$ and a morphism $f \colon C \to A$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, we must exhibit a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = \alpha \circ g$. \emph{Existence}: take $g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$. Then $\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$, which equals $f$ precisely when $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. We obtain this equality by descent from the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$. Base-change the data along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$: the curve $C_{\bar k}$ is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible of genus $0$ (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve), the group scheme $A_{\bar k}$ is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible, the base-changed point $P_{\bar k}$ is a $\bar k$-rational point, and $f_{\bar k}$ still kills it: $f_{\bar k}(P_{\bar k}) = \eta_{A_{\bar k}}$. By \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, now carrying $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$), $f_{\bar k}$ equals the constant morphism $\mathtt{toUnit}\,C_{\bar k} \circ \eta_{A_{\bar k}}$ over $\bar k$. Finally we descend this morphism equality back to $k$: the projection $p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C$ is the base change of the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$, hence faithfully flat and surjective, hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; right-cancelling $p$ against the base-change-square identities yields $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$ as $k$-scheme morphisms (Sub-step~C.2.f of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}). \emph{Uniqueness}: any two morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to A$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ agreeing on $P \circ (-) = \eta_A$ are equal by the universal property of the terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ as a category (a morphism out of $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ is determined by where it sends the unique generator, here pinned to $\eta_A$).
475

534	\section{Implementation route via the Albanese functor}
535	
536	Because the full Picard-scheme representability theorem is not available in current Mathlib, the formal construction proceeds in two layers:
537	
538	\begin{enumerate}
539	  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of $C$ is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, carrying the four properties of Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred} (each obtained by projection from the witness). This rigidity is established over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$ via the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (now carrying $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$) and the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii); the conclusion over a general base field $k$ is then obtained by \emph{descending} the over-$\bar k$ morphism equality along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (a short consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). The genus-$0$ arm is packaged as the explicit Lean construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}: the underlying scheme is the terminal object $\Spec k$, the universal morphism in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch is the unique terminal map \texttt{toUnit}\,$C$, and the universal factorisation reduces to \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} over $\bar k$ followed by descent to $k$.

541	  \item \textbf{Layer II --- Abel--Jacobi compatibility.} Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} introduces the Abel--Jacobi morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ and shows that it is universal among morphisms from $C$ to abelian varieties.
542	\end{enumerate}

\item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Key classical input: morphisms from $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- image of dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by the classical fact that, for an abelian variety $A$ of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant. The project ranks three proof routes for this fact; route~(c) is \textbf{committed}, with (b) and (a) as fallbacks.
      \begin{itemize}
        \item[\textbf{(c) [committed; characteristic-free]}] \emph{Theorem of the cube $\to$ rigidity lemma.} $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is a nonsingular, unirational (indeed rational) variety, so every rational map $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \dashrightarrow A$ to an abelian variety is constant by \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant} (Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.10); a morphism is in particular a rational map, so $f$ is constant. The cited proposition rests on \cref{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends} (Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined), \cref{cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp} (Corollary~1.5), \cref{lem:rigidity_theorem} (the Rigidity Theorem~1.1), and \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube} (the theorem of the cube). This chain uses \emph{neither} $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre duality \emph{nor} the Picard representability engine, and is valid in arbitrary characteristic. It is the route the project commits to for \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}; see \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}.

        \item[\textbf{(b) [fallback]}] \emph{Via the dual abelian variety.} Pullback of line bundles along $f$ induces a homomorphism of group schemes $f^{\vee} \colon A^{\vee} \to \Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$, where $A^{\vee}$ is the dual abelian variety (the moduli space of degree-zero line bundles on $A$). The Picard group of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ has trivial degree-zero component, $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, because every line bundle on $\mathbb P^1$ of degree $0$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}$ (Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6 and II.7). Hence $f^{\vee} = 0$. By autoduality of abelian varieties ($A^{\vee\vee} = A$ canonically, double duality of the Poincar\'e line bundle), the zero of $f^{\vee}$ implies that $f$ itself is constant. This route is free once Route~A's $\Pic^0$ engine exists, but it \emph{couples} the genus-$0$ rigidity to the Route~A representability monster, which the committed route~(c) deliberately avoids.

        \item[\textbf{(a) [fallback; off the critical path; carries a characteristic-$p$ gap]}] \emph{Via the cotangent bundle (the differential $df = 0$ route).} The cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ of an abelian variety is a trivial $\mathcal O_A$-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along $f$ yields a morphism of locally free sheaves $f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}$, whose global sections form a $\bar k$-linear map
        \[
          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
        \]
        Triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ means the left-hand side is a non-zero $\bar k$-vector space (of dimension $g$); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential $df$ to vanish identically on $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. Smoothness of $A$ over $\bar k$ together with the vanishing of $df$ forces $f$ to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism. This is the \emph{differential} route: it requires the Serre-duality-flavoured vanishing $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$ together with the cotangent-triviality of $\Omega_A$, is retained in the tree via the closed chart-algebra envelope (which supplies only the converse implication $df = 0 \Rightarrow f$ constant), and carries a characteristic-$p$ gap. It is \emph{not} the committed route.
      \end{itemize}
      Each route yields: $f$ is constant. The pointed condition $f(p) = \eta_A$ then identifies the constant value, so $f = c$ on underlying topological points.

input.new_string:
\subsection{Route (c): rigidity via the theorem of the cube (committed genus-$0$ proof route)}
\label{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}

The genus-$0$ witness \emph{object} is the trivial scheme $\Spec k$; the only remaining mathematical content of the genus-$0$ arm is the rigidity \emph{statement} \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}. The project commits to proving it by the characteristic-free \emph{theorem-of-the-cube $\to$ rigidity-lemma} chain of Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, recorded in the blocks below. The headline fact the genus-$0$ arm consumes is \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}, the specialisation to a genus-$0$ curve of \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant} (Milne, Proposition~3.10), applied to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (a rational, hence unirational, variety). This chain uses \emph{neither} $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre duality \emph{nor} the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A. The same rigidity machinery is what the positive-genus Albanese universal property (Milne, Propositions~6.1/6.4) needs anyway, here used independently of the Picard representability.

\begin{theorem}
[Theorem of the cube]
  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 5.1 (Theorem of the Cube), \S I.5, p.~21 (PDF p.~27)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 5.1 (THEOREM OF THE CUBE). Let $U$, $V$, $W$ be
  % complete geometrically irreducible varieties over $k$, and let
  % $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, $w_0 \in W(k)$ be base points. Then an
  % invertible sheaf $L$ on $U \times V \times W$ is trivial if its
  % restrictions to $U \times V \times \{w_0\}$, $U \times \{v_0\} \times W$,
  % $\{u_0\} \times V \times W$ are all trivial."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We defer the proof until later in this section
  % [actually, until the next version]." (proof deferred in the source notes)
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 5.1.}
  Let $U$, $V$, $W$ be complete geometrically irreducible varieties over $k$ with base points $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, $w_0 \in W(k)$. An invertible sheaf $L$ on $U \times V \times W$ is trivial as soon as its restrictions to the three coordinate faces $U \times V \times \{w_0\}$, $U \times \{v_0\} \times W$, and $\{u_0\} \times V \times W$ are all trivial.
\end{theorem}

\begin{lemma}
[Rigidity Theorem]
  \label{lem:rigidity_theorem}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 1.1 (Rigidity Theorem), \S I.1, p.~8 (PDF p.~14--15)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 1.1 (RIGIDITY THEOREM). Consider a regular map
  % $\alpha\colon V \times W \to U$, and assume that $V$ is complete and that
  % $V \times W$ is geometrically irreducible. If there are points
  % $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, and $w_0 \in W(k)$ such that
  % $\alpha(V \times \{w_0\}) = \{u_0\} = \alpha(\{v_0\} \times W)$
  % then $\alpha(V \times W) = \{u_0\}$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Since the hypotheses continue to hold after
  % extending scalars from $k$ to $k^{al}$, we can assume $k$ is
  % algebraically closed. Note that $V$ is connected, because otherwise
  % $V \times_k W$ wouldn't be connected, much less irreducible. We need to
  % use the following facts: (i) If $V$ is complete, then the projection map
  % $q\colon V \times_k W \to W$ is closed (this is the definition of being
  % complete AG 7.1). (ii) If $V$ is complete and connected, and
  % $\varphi\colon V \to U$ is a regular map from $V$ into an affine variety,
  % then $\varphi(V) = \{$ point$\}$ (AG 7.5). Let $U_0$ be an open affine
  % neighbourhood of $u_0$. Because of (i), $Z =_{df} q(\alpha^{-1}(U
  % \smallsetminus U_0))$ is closed in $W$. By definition, $Z$ consists of the
  % second coordinates of points of $V \times W$ not mapping into $U_0$. Thus a
  % point $w$ of $W$ lies outside $Z$ if and only $\alpha(V \times \{w\})
  % \subseteq U_0$. In particular $w_0$ lies outside $Z$, and so $W
  % \smallsetminus Z$ is nonempty. As $V \times \{w\}$ is complete and $U_0$ is
  % affine, $\alpha(V \times \{w\})$ must be a point whenever $w \in W
  % \smallsetminus Z$: in fact, $\alpha(V \times \{w\}) = \alpha(v_0,w) =
  % \{u_0\}$. Thus $\alpha$ is constant on the subset $V \times (W
  % \smallsetminus Z)$ of $V \times W$. As $V \times (W \smallsetminus Z)$ is
  % nonempty and open in $V \times W$, and $V \times W$ is irreducible,
  % $V \times (W \smallsetminus Z)$ is dense $V \times W$. As $U$ is separated,
  % $\alpha$ must agree with the constant map on the whole of $V \times W$."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 1.1.}
  \uses{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  Let $\alpha \colon V \times W \to U$ be a regular map with $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically irreducible. If there are points $u_0 \in U(k)$, $v_0 \in V(k)$, $w_0 \in W(k)$ with $\alpha(V \times \{w_0\}) = \{u_0\} = \alpha(\{v_0\} \times W)$, then $\alpha(V \times W) = \{u_0\}$. (If the two coordinate axes collapse to a point, the whole product collapses to that point.)
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:rigidity_theorem}
  After extending scalars to $\bar k = k^{al}$ (the hypotheses persist), assume $k$ algebraically closed; $V$ is then connected. Let $U_0$ be an open affine neighbourhood of $u_0$. By completeness of $V$ the projection $q \colon V \times W \to W$ is closed, so $Z := q(\alpha^{-1}(U \smallsetminus U_0))$ is closed in $W$, and $w \notin Z \iff \alpha(V \times \{w\}) \subseteq U_0$. Since $w_0 \notin Z$, the open set $W \smallsetminus Z$ is non-empty; for $w \in W \smallsetminus Z$ the image $\alpha(V \times \{w\})$ is a complete subset of the affine $U_0$, hence a single point, namely $\alpha(v_0, w) = u_0$. Thus $\alpha$ is constant on the dense open $V \times (W \smallsetminus Z)$ of the irreducible $V \times W$; as $U$ is separated, $\alpha$ agrees with the constant map $u_0$ on all of $V \times W$.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}
[Maps out of a product of complete varieties to an abelian variety]
  \label{cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.5, \S I.1, p.~9 (PDF p.~15--16)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.5. Let $V$ and $W$ be complete varieties over
  % $k$ with $k$-rational points $v_0$ and $w_0$, and let $p$ and $q$ be the
  % projection maps $V \times W \to V$ and $V \times W \to W$. Let $A$ be an
  % abelian variety. Then a morphism $h\colon V \times W \to A$ such that
  % $h(v_0,w_0) = 0$ can be written uniquely as $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$
  % with $f\colon V \to A$ and $g\colon W \to A$ morphisms such that
  % $f(v_0) = 0$ and $g(w_0) = 0$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Set $f = h|V \times \{w_0\}$, $g = h|\{v_0\} \times
  % W$;" (proof opening, p.~15; remainder on PDF p.~16 not transcribed here ---
  % it applies the Rigidity Theorem 1.1 to the difference $h - f\circ p -
  % g\circ q$, which vanishes on both coordinate axes.)
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary 1.5.}
  \uses{lem:rigidity_theorem}
  Let $V$, $W$ be complete varieties over $k$ with $k$-rational points $v_0$, $w_0$, with projections $p \colon V \times W \to V$, $q \colon V \times W \to W$, and let $A$ be an abelian variety. Every morphism $h \colon V \times W \to A$ with $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$ decomposes uniquely as $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$ with $f \colon V \to A$, $g \colon W \to A$ satisfying $f(v_0) = 0$ and $g(w_0) = 0$. The decomposition follows by applying \cref{lem:rigidity_theorem} to $h - f\circ p - g\circ q$, which kills both coordinate axes.
\end{corollary}

\begin{lemma}
[Rational maps from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extend]
  \label{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 3.2, \S I.3, p.~16 (PDF p.~23)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map $\alpha\colon V \dashrightarrow A$
  % from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of
  % $V$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
  % (Theorem 3.1: a rational map from a normal variety to a complete variety is
  % defined off a closed subset of codimension $\geq 2$, p.~16; Lemma 3.3: for a
  % rational map to a group variety the indeterminacy locus, if non-empty, is
  % pure of codimension 1, p.~17.)
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem 3.2.}
  A rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$ from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of $V$ (a genuine morphism). Combine Milne's Theorem~3.1 (rational maps from a normal variety to a complete variety are defined off codimension~$\geq 2$) with Lemma~3.3 (for a rational map to a group variety the indeterminacy locus, if non-empty, is pure of codimension~$1$); the two are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proposition}
[Rational maps from a unirational variety to an abelian variety are constant]
  \label{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10, \S I.3, p.~20 (PDF p.~26)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha\colon V
  % \dashrightarrow A$ from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is
  % constant."
  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically
  % closed field is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map
  % $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can
  % be embedded into $k(X_1,\ldots,X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A
  % variety $V$ over an arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if
  % $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By
  % assumption there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense
  % image, and the composite of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism
  % $\beta\colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction
  % argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are regular maps
  % $\beta_i\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1,\ldots,x_d) = \sum
  % \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition 3.10.}
  \uses{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends, cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp}
  A variety $V$ is \emph{unirational} if (over $\bar k$) there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$ from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant, cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp, lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}
  We may assume $k$ algebraically closed. The dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ composed with $\alpha$ extends (by \cref{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}, after compactifying each factor) to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction starting from \cref{cor:complete_product_to_AV_decomp} writes $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ for regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; the one-factor case (a morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$, killed by the Rigidity Theorem applied to its difference of values) shows each $\beta_i$ is constant, hence $\beta$, hence $\alpha$, is constant.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}
[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 specialised to $\mathbb P^1$, \S I.3, p.~20 (PDF p.~26)  (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha\colon V
  % \dashrightarrow A$ from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is
  % constant."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition 3.10 (specialised to $\mathbb P^1$).}
  \uses{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant, lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends}
  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field, $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and $A$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $f(p) = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point $p$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. This is the project's committed proof route (route~(c)) for \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant, lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends, prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve has a $\bar k$-rational point and is therefore isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, which is a rational --- hence unirational --- nonsingular variety. The morphism $f$ is in particular a rational map $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \dashrightarrow A$, so \cref{prop:unirational_to_AV_constant} (Milne, Proposition~3.10) shows $f$ is constant; \cref{lem:rational_map_to_AV_extends} guarantees that there is no indeterminacy to worry about (the rational map is everywhere defined). The pointed hypothesis $f(p) = \eta_A$ pins the constant value to $\eta_A$, so $f$ equals the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. The argument invokes no differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre duality, and no Picard representability, and is valid in arbitrary characteristic --- once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis currently carried by \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} be dropped.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}\leanok
[Genus-$0$ Albanese witness]
  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
